title: Photo Documentation for Water Damage Insurance Claims — What to Shoot, Metadata & Orientation Mapping
description: Learn the photo documentation standards required for Australian water damage insurance claims — what to photograph, how to organise by room orientation, metadata requirements, and common mistakes that cause claim disputes. RestoreAssist structures photo documentation automatically.
author: Phill McGurk | RestoreAssist
transcript:
## Why Photo Documentation is Critical

In Australian water damage insurance claims, photographs are the primary evidentiary record of the damage as it existed at the time of the technician's first inspection. Once restoration work begins, the pre-works condition is destroyed. If the photo documentation is inadequate, the insurer has no basis to verify that the claimed damage existed at the time of loss — which directly impacts payment.

Strong photo documentation is also the most effective tool for preventing scope disputes. An adjuster who can see each damaged item in context, with a moisture reading visible in the frame, has little basis to dispute its inclusion in the scope.

## The Core Documentation Set

Every water damage job should have the following minimum photo set:

**Establishment shots (3–5 photos):**
- Building exterior showing the structure in context (property address visible or neighbouring structures identifiable)
- Source of loss (burst pipe, failed appliance, etc.) with the failed component visible
- Overview of affected area before any work commences

**Room documentation (per affected room):**
- Four-corner shots: one photo from each corner of the room looking toward the opposite corner. These four images together provide complete coverage of every wall surface.
- Source-to-damage continuity: a series of photos showing the path of water travel from the source to the affected area
- Close-up of each damaged material with a moisture reading visible in frame
- Material identification photos for any item to be removed — full view and close-up showing the damage

**Moisture documentation:**
- Each moisture reading photo should show the meter display clearly, with the probe position on the material visible
- Include a reference object (ruler, chalk mark, tape flag) to allow the viewer to identify the exact reading location

## Metadata Requirements

All photos submitted with an insurance claim should have reliable EXIF metadata, particularly:
- **Date and time**: Confirms when the photo was taken, establishing the timeline of inspection and damage progression
- **GPS coordinates** (where available): Confirms the site location
- **Camera model**: Establishes the provenance of the image

Do not alter the date/time settings on a phone or camera used for claims documentation. Photos with timestamps that contradict the claimed attendance timeline are a serious red flag for adjusters and potentially a fraud indicator.

If using a dedicated claims documentation app, the app should automatically embed location and timestamp data into each image.

## Orientation Mapping

Orientation mapping links each photo to a specific location and direction on the floor plan. This allows an adjuster — or a court — to precisely locate where every photo was taken without visiting the site.

Minimum orientation information for each photo:
- Room name
- Wall face (North, South, East, West — or compass equivalent relative to building entry)
- Height at which the photo was taken (floor level, mid-wall, ceiling)
- The sequence number (e.g., photo 14 of 47)

In practice, this is achieved by maintaining a photo log keyed to a numbered floor plan, or by using documentation software that geotags photos to a floor plan canvas.

## Common Documentation Failures

- Photos taken after protective covers or tarps have been laid (damage obscured)
- No source-of-loss photo (adjuster cannot confirm the cause)
- Missing close-ups with moisture readings (scope can be disputed item by item)
- Photos stored by date only with no room or location reference
- Metadata stripped by cloud backup services (use original files for claim submission)
videoScript:
G'day, welcome to RestoreAssist. Today I'm going to walk you through photo documentation for water damage insurance claims — what to shoot, how to organise it, and the mistakes that will come back to haunt you.

Here's the thing about water damage: the moment you start work, you're destroying the evidence. The pre-works photos are the only record of the damage as it existed when you arrived. If those photos aren't complete, you have no basis to defend your scope against a challenge.

So let's cover the minimum photo set.

Establishment shots first. Get the building exterior — ideally with the address or a neighbouring property visible for context. Get the source of loss. If it's a burst pipe, I want to see the failed pipe and fitting. If it's a washing machine overflow, I want to see the machine and the failed hose. Source documentation is something a lot of technicians skip, and adjusters notice.

Then for each affected room, the four-corner technique. Stand in each corner of the room and shoot toward the opposite corner. Four photos, and between them you've covered every wall surface in that room. It's simple, fast, and leaves nothing out.

For each material that's going into your scope as damaged — especially anything being removed and replaced — you need a close-up with a moisture reading visible in the frame. Put the meter probe on the material, show the reading on the display, and shoot the photo. One image proves the material is wet and that the moisture meter confirmed it. An adjuster cannot dispute a line item that has that photo behind it.

Now, metadata. Every photo you take has a timestamp embedded in it by the camera or phone. That timestamp confirms when you were on site. Do not adjust the date or time settings on your documentation device — ever. A photo claiming to show day-one damage that has a timestamp from three days after the reported loss is a fraud indicator, even if it's a genuine mistake.

And orientation mapping — link your photos to a floor plan. Each photo should be tagged with the room, the wall face it's showing, and its position in the photo sequence. That's what allows an adjuster to reconstruct the damage story without visiting the site.

In RestoreAssist, you attach photos directly to specific points on the floor plan canvas, and the system embeds the location reference automatically. Your photo index is built as you shoot.

Head to restoreassist.app to see how it works.
